title: We May Owe Our Intelligence to Our Unique Neurons
gist: A PNAS study suggests human intelligence may stem not from brain size or neuron count, which are only modestly larger than in great apes, but from the unique structure of individual human cortical neurons.

insights.claim: Human brains are not especially large in absolute terms and pack in only modestly more neurons than great apes, not orders of magnitude more, challenging size-based explanations of human intelligence.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Undercuts the intuitive brain-size story of human cognition.
insights.locator: Considering the spectrum of brain sizes

insights.claim: A PNAS study proposes human intelligence may owe more to the unique structure of individual cortical neurons than to overall brain size or neuron count.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Shifts the search for the basis of intelligence to cellular architecture.
insights.locator: we might owe our marvelous brains

top_entities: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
